verses.latin_vulgate: Ut autem judicatum est navigare eum in Italiam, et tradi Paulum cum reliquis custodiis centurioni nomine Julio cohortis Augustæ,
verses.greek_textus_receptus: ὡς δὲ ἐκρίθη τοῦ ἀποπλεῖν ἡμᾶς εἰς τὴν ἰταλίαν παρεδίδουν τόν τε παῦλον καί τινας ἑτέρους δεσμώτας ἑκατοντάρχῃ ὀνόματι ἰουλίῳ σπείρης σεβαστῆς
verses.king_james: And when it was determined that we should sail into Italy, they delivered Paul and certain other prisoners unto *one* named Julius, a centurion of Augustus' band.

verses.latin_vulgate: ascendentes navem Adrumetinam, incipientes navigare circa Asiæ loca, sustulimus, perseverante nobiscum Aristarcho Macedone Thessalonicensi.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: ἐπιβάντες δὲ πλοίῳ ἀδραμυττηνῷ μέλλοντες πλεῖν τοὺς κατὰ τὴν ἀσίαν τόπους ἀνήχθημεν ὄντος σὺν ἡμῖν ἀριστάρχου μακεδόνος θεσσαλονικέως
verses.king_james: And entering into a ship of Adramyttium, we launched, meaning to sail by the coasts of Asia; *one* Aristarchus, a Macedonian of Thessalonica, being with us.

verses.latin_vulgate: Sequenti autem die devenimus Sidonem. Humane autem tractans Julius Paulum, permisit ad amicos ire, et curam sui agere.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: τῇ τε ἑτέρᾳ κατήχθημεν εἰς σιδῶνα φιλανθρώπως τε ὁ ἰούλιος τῷ παύλῳ χρησάμενος ἐπέτρεψεν πρὸς φίλους πορευθέντα ἐπιμελείας τυχεῖν
verses.king_james: And the next *day* we touched at Sidon. And Julius courteously entreated Paul, and gave *him* liberty to go unto his friends to refresh himself.

verses.latin_vulgate: Et inde cum sustulissemus, subnavigavimus Cyprum, propterea quod essent venti contrarii.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: κἀκεῖθεν ἀναχθέντες ὑπεπλεύσαμεν τὴν κύπρον διὰ τὸ τοὺς ἀνέμους εἶναι ἐναντίους
verses.king_james: And when we had launched from thence, we sailed under Cyprus, because the winds were contrary.

verses.latin_vulgate: Et pelagus Ciliciæ et Pamphyliæ navigantes, venimus Lystram, quæ est Lyciæ:
verses.greek_textus_receptus: τό τε πέλαγος τὸ κατὰ τὴν κιλικίαν καὶ παμφυλίαν διαπλεύσαντες κατήλθομεν εἰς μύρα τῆς λυκίας
verses.king_james: And when we had sailed over the sea of Cilicia and Pamphylia, we came to Myra, *a city* of Lycia.

verses.latin_vulgate: et ibi inveniens centurio navem Alexandrinam navigantem in Italiam, transposuit nos in eam.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: κἀκεῖ εὑρὼν ὁ ἑκατόνταρχος πλοῖον ἀλεξανδρῖνον πλέον εἰς τὴν ἰταλίαν ἐνεβίβασεν ἡμᾶς εἰς αὐτό
verses.king_james: And there the centurion found a ship of Alexandria sailing into Italy; and he put us therein.

verses.latin_vulgate: Et cum multis diebus tarde navigaremus, et vix devenissemus contra Gnidum, prohibente nos vento, adnavigavimus Cretæ juxta Salmonem:
verses.greek_textus_receptus: ἐν ἱκαναῖς δὲ ἡμέραις βραδυπλοοῦντες καὶ μόλις γενόμενοι κατὰ τὴν κνίδον μὴ προσεῶντος ἡμᾶς τοῦ ἀνέμου ὑπεπλεύσαμεν τὴν κρήτην κατὰ σαλμώνην
verses.king_james: And when we had sailed slowly many days, and scarce were come over against Cnidus, the wind not suffering us, we sailed under Crete, over against Salmone;

verses.latin_vulgate: et vix juxta navigantes, venimus in locum quemdam qui vocatur Boniportus, cui juxta erat civitas Thalassa.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: μόλις τε παραλεγόμενοι αὐτὴν ἤλθομεν εἰς τόπον τινὰ καλούμενον καλοὺς λιμένας ᾧ ἐγγὺς ἦν πόλις λασαία
verses.king_james: And, hardly passing it, came unto a place which is called The fair havens; nigh whereunto was the city *of* Lasea.

verses.latin_vulgate: Multo autem tempore peracto, et cum jam non esset tuta navigatio eo quod et jejunium jam præteriisset, consolabatur eos Paulus,
verses.greek_textus_receptus: ἱκανοῦ δὲ χρόνου διαγενομένου καὶ ὄντος ἤδη ἐπισφαλοῦς τοῦ πλοὸς διὰ τὸ καὶ τὴν νηστείαν ἤδη παρεληλυθέναι παρῄνει ὁ παῦλος
verses.king_james: Now when much time was spent, and when sailing was now dangerous, because the fast was now already past, Paul admonished *them*,

verses.latin_vulgate: dicens eis: Viri, video quoniam cum injuria et multo damno non solum oneris, et navis, sed etiam animarum nostrarum incipit esse navigatio.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: λέγων αὐτοῖς, ἄνδρες θεωρῶ ὅτι μετὰ ὕβρεως καὶ πολλῆς ζημίας οὐ μόνον τοῦ φόρτου καὶ τοῦ πλοίου ἀλλὰ καὶ τῶν ψυχῶν ἡμῶν μέλλειν ἔσεσθαι τὸν πλοῦν
verses.king_james: And said unto them, Sirs, I perceive that this voyage will be with hurt and much damage, not only of the lading and ship, but also of our lives.

verses.latin_vulgate: Centurio autem gubernatori et nauclero magis credebat, quam his quæ a Paulo dicebantur.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: ὁ δὲ ἑκατόνταρχος τῷ κυβερνήτῃ καὶ τῷ ναυκλήρῳ ἐπείθετο μᾶλλον ἢ τοῖς ὑπὸ τοῦ παύλου λεγομένοις
verses.king_james: Nevertheless the centurion believed the master and the owner of the ship, more than those things which were spoken by Paul.

verses.latin_vulgate: Et cum aptus portus non esset ad hiemandum, plurimi statuerunt consilium navigare inde, si quomodo possent, devenientes Phœnicen hiemare, portum Cretæ respicientem ad Africum et ad Corum.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: ἀνευθέτου δὲ τοῦ λιμένος ὑπάρχοντος πρὸς παραχειμασίαν οἱ πλείους ἔθεντο βουλὴν ἀναχθῆναι κἀκεῖθεν εἴπως δύναιντο καταντήσαντες εἰς φοίνικα παραχειμάσαι λιμένα τῆς κρήτης βλέποντα κατὰ λίβα καὶ κατὰ χῶρον
verses.king_james: And because the haven was not commodious to winter in, the more part advised to depart thence also, if by any means they might attain to Phenice, *and there* to winter; *which is* an haven of Crete, and lieth toward the south west and north west.

verses.latin_vulgate: Aspirante autem austro, æstimantes propositum se tenere, cum sustulissent de Asson, legebant Cretam.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: ὑποπνεύσαντος δὲ νότου δόξαντες τῆς προθέσεως κεκρατηκέναι ἄραντες ἆσσον παρελέγοντο τὴν κρήτην
verses.king_james: And when the south wind blew softly, supposing that they had obtained *their* purpose, loosing *thence*, they sailed close by Crete.

verses.latin_vulgate: Non post multum autem misit se contra ipsam ventus typhonicus, qui vocatur Euroaquilo.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: μετ᾽ οὐ πολὺ δὲ ἔβαλεν κατ᾽ αὐτῆς ἄνεμος τυφωνικὸς ὁ καλούμενος εὐροκλύδων
verses.king_james: But not long after there arose against it a tempestuous wind, called Euroclydon.

verses.latin_vulgate: Cumque arrepta esset navis, et non posset conari in ventum, data nave flatibus, ferebamur.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: συναρπασθέντος δὲ τοῦ πλοίου καὶ μὴ δυναμένου ἀντοφθαλμεῖν τῷ ἀνέμῳ ἐπιδόντες ἐφερόμεθα
verses.king_james: And when the ship was caught, and could not bear up into the wind, we let *her* drive.

verses.latin_vulgate: In insulam autem quamdam decurrentes, quæ vocatur Cauda, potuimus vix obtinere scapham.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: νησίον δέ τι ὑποδραμόντες καλούμενον κλαύδην, μόλις ἰσχύσαμεν περικρατεῖς γενέσθαι τῆς σκάφης
verses.king_james: And running under a certain island which is called Clauda, we had much work to come by the boat:

verses.latin_vulgate: Qua sublata, adjutoriis utebantur, accingentes navem, timentes ne in Syrtim inciderent, summisso vase sic ferebantur.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: ἣν ἄραντες βοηθείαις ἐχρῶντο ὑποζωννύντες τὸ πλοῖον φοβούμενοί τε μὴ εἰς τὴν σύρτιν ἐκπέσωσιν χαλάσαντες τὸ σκεῦος οὕτως ἐφέροντο
verses.king_james: Which when they had taken up, they used helps, undergirding the ship; and, fearing lest they should fall into the quicksands, strake sail, and so were driven.

verses.latin_vulgate: Valida autem nobis tempestate jactatis, sequenti die jactum fecerunt:
verses.greek_textus_receptus: σφοδρῶς δὲ χειμαζομένων ἡμῶν τῇ ἑξῆς ἐκβολὴν ἐποιοῦντο
verses.king_james: And we being exceedingly tossed with a tempest, the next *day* they lightened the ship;

verses.latin_vulgate: et tertia die suis manibus armamenta navis projecerunt.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: καὶ τῇ τρίτῃ αὐτόχειρες τὴν σκευὴν τοῦ πλοίου ἔῤῥιψαμεν
verses.king_james: And the third *day* we cast out with our own hands the tackling of the ship.

verses.latin_vulgate: Neque autem sole, neque sideribus apparentibus per plures dies, et tempestate non exigua imminente, jam ablata erat spes omnis salutis nostræ.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: μήτε δὲ ἡλίου μήτε ἄστρων ἐπιφαινόντων ἐπὶ πλείονας ἡμέρας χειμῶνός τε οὐκ ὀλίγου ἐπικειμένου λοιπὸν περιῃρεῖτο πᾶσα ἐλπὶς τοῦ σῴζεσθαι ἡμᾶς
verses.king_james: And when neither sun nor stars in many days appeared, and no small tempest lay on *us*, all hope that we should be saved was then taken away.

verses.latin_vulgate: Et cum multa jejunatio fuisset, tunc stans Paulus in medio eorum, dixit: Oportebat quidem, o viri, audito me, non tollere a Creta, lucrique facere injuriam hanc et jacturam.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: πολλῆς δέ ἀσιτίας ὑπαρχούσης τότε σταθεὶς ὁ παῦλος ἐν μέσῳ αὐτῶν εἶπεν ἔδει μέν ὦ ἄνδρες πειθαρχήσαντάς μοι μὴ ἀνάγεσθαι ἀπὸ τῆς κρήτης κερδῆσαί τε τὴν ὕβριν ταύτην καὶ τὴν ζημίαν
verses.king_james: But after long abstinence Paul stood forth in the midst of them, and said, Sirs, ye should have hearkened unto me, and not have loosed from Crete, and to have gained this harm and loss.

verses.latin_vulgate: Et nunc suadeo vobis bono animo esse: amissio enim nullius animæ erit ex vobis, præterquam navis.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: καὶ τανῦν παραινῶ ὑμᾶς εὐθυμεῖν ἀποβολὴ γὰρ ψυχῆς οὐδεμία ἔσται ἐξ ὑμῶν πλὴν τοῦ πλοίου
verses.king_james: And now I exhort you to be of good cheer: for there shall be no loss of *any man's* life among you, but of the ship.

verses.latin_vulgate: Astitit enim mihi hac nocte angelus Dei, cujus sum ego, et cui deservio,
verses.greek_textus_receptus: παρέστη γάρ μοι τῇ νυκτὶ ταύτῃ ἄγγελος τοῦ θεοῦ οὗ εἰμι ᾧ καὶ λατρεύω
verses.king_james: For there stood by me this night the angel of God, whose I am, and whom I serve,

verses.latin_vulgate: dicens: Ne timeas, Paule: Cæsari te oportet assistere: et ecce donavit tibi Deus omnes qui navigant tecum.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: λέγων, μὴ φοβοῦ παῦλε καίσαρί σε δεῖ παραστῆναι καὶ ἰδού, κεχάρισταί σοι ὁ θεὸς πάντας τοὺς πλέοντας μετὰ σοῦ
verses.king_james: Saying, Fear not, Paul; thou must be brought before Caesar: and, lo, God hath given thee all them that sail with thee.

verses.latin_vulgate: Propter quod bono animo estote, viri: credo enim Deo quia sic erit, quemadmodum dictum est mihi.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: διὸ εὐθυμεῖτε ἄνδρες πιστεύω γὰρ τῷ θεῷ ὅτι οὕτως ἔσται καθ᾽ ὃν τρόπον λελάληταί μοι
verses.king_james: Wherefore, sirs, be of good cheer: for I believe God, that it shall be even as it was told me.

verses.latin_vulgate: In insulam autem quamdam oportet nos devenire.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: εἰς νῆσον δέ τινα δεῖ ἡμᾶς ἐκπεσεῖν
verses.king_james: Howbeit we must be cast upon a certain island.

verses.latin_vulgate: Sed posteaquam quartadecima nox supervenit, navigantibus nobis in Adria circa mediam noctem, suspicabantur nautæ apparere sibi aliquam regionem.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: ὡς δὲ τεσσαρεσκαιδεκάτη νὺξ ἐγένετο διαφερομένων ἡμῶν ἐν τῷ ἀδρίᾳ κατὰ μέσον τῆς νυκτὸς ὑπενόουν οἱ ναῦται προσάγειν τινὰ αὐτοῖς χώραν
verses.king_james: But when the fourteenth night was come, as we were driven up and down in Adria, about midnight the shipmen deemed that they drew near to some country;

verses.latin_vulgate: Qui et summittentes bolidem, invenerunt passus viginti: et pusillum inde separati, invenerunt passus quindecim.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: καὶ βολίσαντες εὗρον ὀργυιὰς εἴκοσι βραχὺ δὲ διαστήσαντες καὶ πάλιν βολίσαντες εὗρον ὀργυιὰς δεκαπέντε
verses.king_james: And sounded, and found *it* twenty fathoms: and when they had gone a little further, they sounded again, and found *it* fifteen fathoms.

verses.latin_vulgate: Timentes autem ne in aspera loca incideremus, de puppi mittentes anchoras quatuor, optabant diem fieri.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: φοβούμενοί τε μήπως εἰς τραχεῖς τόπους ἐκπέσωσιν ἐκ πρύμνης ῥίψαντες ἀγκύρας τέσσαρας ηὔχοντο ἡμέραν γενέσθαι
verses.king_james: Then fearing lest we should have fallen upon rocks, they cast four anchors out of the stern, and wished for the day.

verses.latin_vulgate: Nautis vero quærentibus fugere de navi, cum misissent scapham in mare, sub obtentu quasi inciperent a prora anchoras extendere,
verses.greek_textus_receptus: τῶν δὲ ναυτῶν ζητούντων φυγεῖν ἐκ τοῦ πλοίου καὶ χαλασάντων τὴν σκάφην εἰς τὴν θάλασσαν προφάσει ὡς ἐκ πρώρας μελλόντων ἀγκύρας ἐκτείνειν
verses.king_james: And as the shipmen were about to flee out of the ship, when they had let down the boat into the sea, under colour as though they would have cast anchors out of the foreship,

verses.latin_vulgate: dixit Paulus centurioni et militibus: Nisi hi in navi manserint, vos salvi fieri non potestis.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: εἶπεν ὁ παῦλος τῷ ἑκατοντάρχῃ καὶ τοῖς στρατιώταις ἐὰν μὴ οὗτοι μείνωσιν ἐν τῷ πλοίῳ ὑμεῖς σωθῆναι οὐ δύνασθε
verses.king_james: Paul said to the centurion and to the soldiers, Except these abide in the ship, ye cannot be saved.

verses.latin_vulgate: Tunc absciderunt milites funes scaphæ, et passi sunt eam excidere.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: τότε οἱ στρατιῶται ἀπέκοψαν τὰ σχοινία τῆς σκάφης καὶ εἴασαν αὐτὴν ἐκπεσεῖν
verses.king_james: Then the soldiers cut off the ropes of the boat, and let her fall off.

verses.latin_vulgate: Et cum lux inciperet fieri, rogabat Paulus omnes sumere cibum, dicens: Quartadecima die hodie exspectantes jejuni permanetis, nihil accipientes.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: ἄχρι δὲ οὗ ἔμελλεν ἡμέρα γίνεσθαι παρεκάλει ὁ παῦλος ἅπαντας μεταλαβεῖν τροφῆς λέγων, τεσσαρεσκαιδεκάτην σήμερον ἡμέραν προσδοκῶντες ἄσιτοι διατελεῖτε μηδὲν προσλαβόμενοι
verses.king_james: And while the day was coming on, Paul besought *them* all to take meat, saying, This day is the fourteenth day that ye have tarried and continued fasting, having taken nothing.

verses.latin_vulgate: Propter quod rogo vos accipere cibum pro salute vestra: quia nullius vestrum capillus de capite peribit.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: διὸ παρακαλῶ ὑμᾶς προσλαβεῖν τροφῆς τοῦτο γὰρ πρὸς τῆς ὑμετέρας σωτηρίας ὑπάρχει οὐδενὸς γὰρ ὑμῶν θρὶξ ἐκ τῆς κεφαλῆς πεσεῖται
verses.king_james: Wherefore I pray you to take *some* meat: for this is for your health: for there shall not an hair fall from the head of any of you.

verses.latin_vulgate: Et cum hæc dixisset, sumens panem, gratias egit Deo in conspectu omnium: et cum fregisset, cœpit manducare.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: εἴπων δὲ ταῦτα καὶ λαβὼν ἄρτον εὐχαρίστησεν τῷ θεῷ ἐνώπιον πάντων καὶ κλάσας ἤρξατο ἐσθίειν
verses.king_james: And when he had thus spoken, he took bread, and gave thanks to God in presence of them all: and when he had broken *it*, he began to eat.

verses.latin_vulgate: Animæquiores autem facti omnes, et ipsi sumpserunt cibum.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: εὔθυμοι δὲ γενόμενοι πάντες καὶ αὐτοὶ προσελάβοντο τροφῆς
verses.king_james: Then were they all of good cheer, and they also took *some* meat.

verses.latin_vulgate: Eramus vero universæ animæ in navi ducentæ septuaginta sex.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: ἤμεν δὲ ἐν τῷ πλοίῳ αἱ πᾶσαι ψυχαὶ διακόσιαι ἑβδομήκονταέξ
verses.king_james: And we were in all in the ship two hundred threescore and sixteen souls.

verses.latin_vulgate: Et satiati cibo alleviabant navem, jactantes triticum in mare.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: κορεσθέντες δὲ τροφῆς ἐκούφιζον τὸ πλοῖον ἐκβαλλόμενοι τὸν σῖτον εἰς τὴν θάλασσαν
verses.king_james: And when they had eaten enough, they lightened the ship, and cast out the wheat into the sea.

verses.latin_vulgate: Cum autem dies factus esset, terram non agnoscebant: sinum vero quemdam considerabant habentem littus, in quem cogitabant si possent ejicere navem.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: ὅτε δὲ ἡμέρα ἐγένετο τὴν γῆν οὐκ ἐπεγίνωσκον κόλπον δέ τινα κατενόουν ἔχοντα αἰγιαλὸν εἰς ὃν ἐβουλεύσαντο, εἰ δύναιντο ἐξῶσαι τὸ πλοῖον
verses.king_james: And when it was day, they knew not the land: but they discovered a certain creek with a shore, into the which they were minded, if it were possible, to thrust in the ship.

verses.latin_vulgate: Et cum anchoras sustulissent, committebant se mari, simul laxantes juncturas gubernaculorum: et levato artemone secundum auræ flatum, tendebant ad littus.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: καὶ τὰς ἀγκύρας περιελόντες εἴων εἰς τὴν θάλασσαν ἅμα ἀνέντες τὰς ζευκτηρίας τῶν πηδαλίων καὶ ἐπάραντες τὸν ἀρτέμονα τῇ πνεούσῃ κατεῖχον εἰς τὸν αἰγιαλόν
verses.king_james: And when they had taken up the anchors, they committed *themselves* unto the sea, and loosed the rudder bands, and hoised up the mainsail to the wind, and made toward shore.

verses.latin_vulgate: Et cum incidissemus in locum dithalassum, impegerunt navem: et prora quidem fixa manebat immobilis, puppis vero solvebatur a vi maris.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: περιπεσόντες δὲ εἰς τόπον διθάλασσον ἐπώκειλαν τὴν ναῦν καὶ ἡ μὲν πρῷρα ἐρείσασα ἔμεινεν ἀσάλευτος ἡ δὲ πρύμνα ἐλύετο ὑπὸ τῆς βίας τῶν κυμάτων
verses.king_james: And falling into a place where two seas met, they ran the ship aground; and the forepart stuck fast, and remained unmoveable, but the hinder part was broken with the violence of the waves.

verses.latin_vulgate: Militum autem consilium fuit ut custodias occiderent, ne quis cum enatasset, effugeret.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: τῶν δὲ στρατιωτῶν βουλὴ ἐγένετο ἵνα τοὺς δεσμώτας ἀποκτείνωσιν μήτις ἐκκολυμβήσας διαφύγοι
verses.king_james: And the soldiers' counsel was to kill the prisoners, lest any of them should swim out, and escape.

verses.latin_vulgate: Centurio autem volens servare Paulum, prohibuit fieri: jussitque eos qui possent natare, emittere se primos, et evadere, et ad terram exire:
verses.greek_textus_receptus: ὁ δὲ ἑκατόνταρχος βουλόμενος διασῶσαι τὸν παῦλον ἐκώλυσεν αὐτοὺς τοῦ βουλήματος ἐκέλευσέν τε τοὺς δυναμένους κολυμβᾶν ἀποῤῥίψαντας πρώτους ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν ἐξιέναι
verses.king_james: But the centurion, willing to save Paul, kept them from *their* purpose; and commanded that they which could swim should cast *themselves* first *into the sea*, and get to land:

verses.latin_vulgate: et ceteros, alios in tabulis ferebant, quosdam super ea quæ de navi erant. Et sic factum est, ut omnes animæ evaderent ad terram.
verses.greek_textus_receptus: καὶ τοὺς λοιποὺς οὓς μὲν ἐπὶ σανίσιν οὓς δὲ ἐπί τινων τῶν ἀπὸ τοῦ πλοίου καὶ οὕτως ἐγένετο πάντας διασωθῆναι ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν
verses.king_james: And the rest, some on boards, and some on *broken pieces* of the ship. And so it came to pass, that they escaped all safe to land.
